result:
# Notes on the Backwards Road

This book has a peculiar structure: it intends to reverse itself as you move through it, and the language will sometimes fold, sometimes unfold. There will be inversions and palindromes; there will be lines that run backwards, letters that run backwards, a world that runs backwards. The idea is not to trap you, but to invite you to notice that most journeys are made of mirrored steps. You walk forward toward something that you first imagined in reverse, and when you finally arrive, it is already there, in the shape of the road itself.

I say “road,” but I do not mean pavement. I mean a way of going. And I will not call it a “path,” because “path” is too soft and too clean. I mean something that has been worn by feet and hearts and arguments, something that has been made by repetition and refusal. I mean the habit of moving when you do not know where. I mean a track you build by walking backwards and looking ahead at the same time.

In the beginning there is an image: a town at the edge of the world where everyone writes on paper that has been turned upside down, the words inverted by the pressure of their fingertips as they push against the page. The town’s official name is Undisclosed. Its sign is reversed on purpose. It stands crooked like a person caught between laughter and regret. In Undisclosed, the first thing a child learns is not how to hold a pencil. It is how to move backward as naturally as forward. The children are taught to look where they have been, to keep their eyes on the place they are leaving, and to speak to the future in whispers.

I grew up there, in the second half of the third street after the library and before the market, and my mother was a cartographer. She did not draw borders. She drew the outlines of the spaces between thoughts. She mapped the distances between two people who sit beside each other on a bench and do not speak. She drew the shape of a room after someone leaves it. She took care to mark the direction of wind when it is an emotion and when it is air. My mother taught me that memory has roads, and that the roads have echoes.

The echoes of the roads in Undisclosed were not ordinary echoes. They were long and precise, like a musician’s scales that repeat in the body long after the instrument has stopped sounding. The echoes carried the faint smell of apples or old books, the taste of copper when a storm is near, the warmth of a hand that had just let go. The cartographer’s work was to capture those echoes in lines so that others could remember them better. There was a map of an argument, traced in red like lightning, that would bring two friends back to the place where the misunderstanding started, and a map of a love letter, traced in blue, that would help you find the exact spot where the sentence was first misread.

The town’s other profession was palindromes. People who loved symmetry sat at cafés and said sentences that read the same forward and backward, and when they spoke them, they felt as if time had been folded, and the sound of their voice touched both ends at once. Palindromes were a kind of prayer and a way to remember that language had not been invented as a tool of deception, but as a tool of balance. They were not used to lie or to hide. They were used to say something in a way that could not be broken. Some people said “madam,” and it was a polite recognition that a woman has agency. Others said “no lemon,” and it was a small joke about the way people had come to taste the world, and some said “never odd or even,” which was a philosophical statement about the nature of decision.

In Undisclosed, even the clocks were palindromic. The numbers were arranged in a circle like hands that had decided not to leave. The clock did not tick in minutes that counted a day. It ticked in breaths. It took a deep breath and let it go. It measured silence like a cartographer measures a room. This meant that the town sometimes forgot about calendars and instead lived in rhythms that felt like music. The baker made bread when the bread wanted to be made. The gardener planted seeds when the seeds wanted to be planted. The children laughed when laughter was ready, and the old people cried when tears were ready. There was no rush, because time itself had been folded.

It was not always beautiful. There were hard days in Undisclosed. The town sat between two mountains, and sometimes one of the mountains would speak in a low voice that felt like a storm inside the bones. The town’s library was on fire once, and people saved books by pulling them backward out of the flame, not by stepping forward. The library burned slowly and became ash, and the ash gathered in the shape of a door that would not open unless you read a palindrome and put your hand inside the letter “M,” and said something like “I am not afraid of being wrong.” People wrote on the walls that they wanted to rebuild, and they did, but they rebuilt by writing backwards, so the new library had the feel of a cave, and the words hung like stalactites.

My mother’s work required me to carry paper for her. The paper was thick and smelled like ink that had not been used. We would walk backward through the streets and listen to the town. My mother’s ears were tuned to the echoes. She could hear a conversation from two days ago in the wind. She could find a child’s cry in the dust at the edge of the fountain. She would stop and place the paper against a wall and press her fingers there, and the paper would record the echo like a photograph records light. It was a strange practice, and it made the townspeople look at us sideways. They would call it “reverse mapping,” and they would say, “You are mapping the future into the past,” and my mother would answer, “I am mapping the present into the past, and the present will follow.”

People in other towns called Undisclosed “the place where language is sick.” They said we invented words that do not move. They said we made it so that a poem cannot end, and they accused us of loving silence more than music. This was an unfair thing to say, but it was said often, and it made the market men sell their apples with pride and their pears with caution. The apples were crisp and bright, and the pears were soft and patient. The apples were loved by people who wanted action, and the pears were loved by people who wanted gentleness. The town accepted both, and it grew in proportion.

The town’s doctors treated injuries that had happened earlier than the present moment. If a person came in with a broken heart, the doctor would ask, “Where did your heart break?” and the person would point backward, and the doctor would take the invisible thread that connected the person to that broken place and would fix it, so the person could move again without the weight of what had not happened yet. It was not magic. It was attention. It was the belief that if you hold a memory carefully and put a stitch where the memory was torn, it will stop bleeding, and your blood will stop going backward instead of forward.

I began writing on my own when I was old enough to feel lonely. Loneliness in Undisclosed had a particular taste. It tasted like the edge of the paper when you fold it and tear it carefully with your fingernail. It tasted like the sound of a pencil moving backward on the paper and leaving a faint line that will disappear if you touch it too often. I wrote about the way a person sits at a café when the café is empty. I wrote about the way the empty café holds the memory of conversations as if it were a chest holding books and keys and a photograph of a child playing with a toy that has been lost. I wrote about the way a table remembers the shape of a hand when the hand has already moved on to something else.

I wrote about the physics of longing, which is a science that is never taught in schools, because longing is not considered a serious subject. Longing is a force that moves things without being named. Longing pulls you toward a person who has not spoken your name in years, and it pulls you toward a place you have not visited, and it pulls you toward a version of yourself that you were in a time before you knew how to be careful. It is an invisible railway that nobody pays to ride. If you sit on it, it will take you where you need to go, and if you forget to get off, it will take you past your stop and then back, and then you will have to walk. That is the physics of longing: it is generous and careless.

In Undisclosed, we had a kind of prayer that took the form of palindromes and inverted writing. We wrote “I have loved before,” and then we wrote “fored loved I evah I,” and then we wrote them together on a piece of paper and folded it until the edges met and the crease became a secret. People believed that the secret would be safer that way, and the town was built on the belief that secrets are not evil but necessary, and that the act of keeping a secret is a way of saying that the truth has to be protected from those who would use it carelessly.

When I was sixteen, I fell in love with a woman named Maris. Maris had a voice like rain on a metal roof, and she wrote palindromes on the corners of notebooks and the corners of sidewalks. She said “madam, I’m adam,” and it made people laugh, and “reviver,” and it made people think, and “rotavator,” and it made people look at her with a kind of awe because “rotavator” is not common, and she made it feel like a flower that opens to the sun and closes to the moon. We walked backward together through the market and she would say, “I am going to love you even though love is a backwards road.” She believed that love is a road that moves toward a place you cannot describe, and that the only way to follow it is to trust that your feet know what the road is for.

Maris taught me how to fold time. She taught me to sit in a room that had been empty for years, and to press my palm against the center of the table, and to feel the presence of people who had sat there, and to let that presence become a memory that belonged to me. She taught me to write a sentence that could be read forward and backward, and to say it, and to hear the echoes in the room change. She taught me to love without making promises, because a promise can be broken in the same way that a sentence can be written in reverse without losing meaning.

We spent a summer reading palindromes to each other while the apples fell from the tree in the courtyard, and the pears ripened in the heat, and the baker sang while he kneaded dough, and the old clock counted breaths and not minutes. We spoke softly so as not to scare the echoes. We wrote words backward and forward and together, and we folded them, and we did not tell anyone what we had written, and we made the town feel more like itself than it had been for years.

But then the town began to leak. The mountains spoke again, and this time they said something that sounded like a warning. A storm that had been waiting outside the gates stepped inside the walls and took the air and made it feel heavy. The town’s clocks stopped counting breaths, and the baker could not sing, and the gardener could not plant seeds because the ground remembered that it was still cold, and the children’s laughter felt thin. The town’s cartographer, my mother, began to hear echoes that did not belong to Undisclosed. She began to map the edges of the world, where roads turned into water and water turned into sky, and where the sky began to say things in a language that was not made for human ears.

My mother said, “The world is reversing itself.” She said it as if reversing itself was a normal thing, and not something to be frightened of. She said, “If we walk backwards, we will meet the future in the past.” And so she began to work with people who had left Undisclosed a long time ago. She called them by their last names, and she wrote their names backward, and she said, “You will come back. The past will meet you halfway.” And they did. They came back as echoes and as memories and as stories that were told to children who did not believe in anything that could not be touched. The town took them in and asked them to sit at the table, and to say a palindrome, and to tell a story that began at the end and moved toward the beginning.

The town’s doctors began to heal not only broken hearts but broken futures. They would look at a person who had been afraid of something that had not yet happened, and they would say, “You are carrying a future as if it were a burden that has already arrived. Let me take that burden and put it in a place where it will not hurt you.” And they would hold the person’s hand and whisper a palindrome and write it backward and forward and fold it into a piece of paper that would be kept in a drawer that would be locked with a small key that the person would lose and find and keep. The town did not feel like a town then. It felt like a body, and the doctors were hands, and the cartographer was a mind, and the people were blood and breath and the old clock was a heart that had decided to beat in a rhythm that would not scare anyone.

One morning, I woke and found that the paper my mother had left on the table was filled with a map that was not drawn in lines but in a pattern that looked like weather. There was a circle in the center that was the town’s library, and there were lines that moved out like wind, and there were marks that were not marks but stories that had been folded into themselves. I could not read it. I could feel it. The map was a kind of palindrome itself. The center held the past and the edges held the future, and the past and the future were the same distance away, and the town would walk both ways at once.

Maris and I decided to leave. Not because we were afraid of the leaking world, but because we wanted to test the town’s belief that walking backward would meet the future in the past. We packed a small bag, and we took a loaf of bread that the baker gave us, and we took a pear that the gardener gave us, and we took a piece of paper that my mother gave us. We wrote a palindrome on the paper together. It was “now I win.” We wrote it backward as well, “nid I won.” We folded it, and we put it in a pocket, and we stepped out the door, and we walked backward into the world.

Walking backward was harder than we had imagined. Your body wants to look ahead, and it wants to see where you are going, and it wants to step forward into light and away from shadow. But we held each other’s hands and moved, one foot behind the other, and we looked at what we had left, and we found that looking at what you have left makes you feel generous. It makes you feel as if you have given something away and that the thing you have given is a part of you that does not need you to carry it anymore. We walked past the library’s ash door, and we saw that the ash had begun to bloom into small plants that smelled like lemon and like rain. We walked past the market and we saw that the apples had grown into a kind of language that could be spoken without words. We walked past the baker and we heard his voice singing a song that had been forgotten and then remembered.

As we moved, we began to understand that the world’s leaking had not been a failure. It had been a way of telling us that walls do not keep things in or out, and that the world’s boundaries are made of stories and not of stone. The town’s cartographer’s map was an invitation. The old clocks were not counting breaths as a way to tell us that time was slow. They were counting breaths as a way to tell us that time is a body, and that a body is best when it is alive, and that life is a rhythm that can be felt and not merely counted.

We reached the edge of the town, which was not a wall but a place where the ground turned into a soft slope that met the sky, and we stopped. We turned, and we walked forward, and the sky met us. It met us like a friend who had been waiting for you and had forgotten how to call your name but had kept the shape of your name in a memory that was strong and kind. We walked into the world, and the world was not strange. It felt familiar. It felt like a palindrome that had been invented when we were small and we had been told that language has power, and that power must be used with care. We walked toward the future, and we found the past waiting for us, and we shook hands with it, and we said, “We are not afraid.” And the past said, “I know.”

We lived in other towns and other places, and we wrote palindromes in cafés and in train stations and in parks that were made of stone and sand. We listened to echoes and we mapped them, and we taught children how to walk backward without falling, and we taught adults how to speak to the future without making promises they could not keep. We discovered that the world was full of people who loved symmetry and who hated it, and we loved both kinds. We learned that the physics of longing is a science that can be taught by telling stories that begin at the end, and by writing backwards, and by folding time. We learned that the map that my mother had left on the table was not a map of a place but a map of a way of moving.

Years later, we returned to Undisclosed. The town’s old clocks had begun to count minutes again, and the baker had begun to sing, and the gardener had begun to plant seeds in the ground that remembered that it was ready for a new kind of growth. The library had been rebuilt by people who had walked backward into the world and had found a piece of ash in the shape of a door, and they had used it as a foundation for new walls and new books. The cartographer’s work was still visible on the street in the form of echoes that had been folded into lines and into stories and into palindromes that were being told by children who did not understand what they were saying and understood more than anyone.

Maris and I sat at the table that had been used by a doctor who had healed a broken future, and we listened to the room. We pressed our palms against the center of the table, and we felt the presence of people who had sat there, and we let that presence become a memory that belonged to us. We took out the piece of paper that had “now I win” written on it, forward and backward, and we unfolded it carefully, and we read it aloud, and the room changed. It became brighter. It became more patient. It became more willing to love.

We did not rebuild Undisclosed. We did not make it into something it had not been. We let it be. We let it be a place where people write in reverse when they need to, and let it be a place where palindromes are used as prayers and as jokes, and let it be a place where the clocks count breaths when the town wants to remember that time is not a master but a friend. We let it be. And we walked backward again, not away this time, but into the town’s stories, and we walked forward again, not out this time, but into the town’s future, and we found that the past and the future had become the same distance away.

In the end, I understood that the town’s belief was not about moving backward or forward. It was about moving with awareness. It was about knowing that language has edges, and that edges are useful when you are building walls, and that edges are useless when you want to hold someone, and that edges can be made into a kind of bridge if you are careful. It was about the physics of longing, which is a science of giving without keeping and keeping without possessing. It was about the cartographer’s work, which is about mapping the spaces between people and not mapping the people themselves. It was about the doctors’ work, which is about healing future wounds by treating them as present wounds, and about the baker’s work, which is about making bread when bread wants to be made, and about the gardener’s work, which is about planting seeds when seeds want to be planted.

It was about palindromes, which are not only sentences but ways of thinking that make room for symmetry and for asymmetry, for order and for the mess that order cannot hold. It was about writing backward and forward and together and folded, and about letting folded things be secret until they become something that can be told without harm. It was about walking backward into the world and forward into the world and discovering that the world is not two things that are different. It is one thing that changes, and that is the power and the danger and the beauty of it.

If you ever come to Undisclosed, do not expect the town to explain itself. Do not expect the town to apologize for its palindromes and its reverse writing and its backward streets. Do not expect the town to tell you how to read its maps. Expect the town to offer you a piece of paper that will record echoes when you press your fingers against the walls. Expect the town to offer you a palindrome that will make you laugh or think. Expect the town to offer you a pear that tastes patient and gentle. Expect the town to say, “Walk backward. Look at where you have been. Then walk forward. Look at where you are going.” Expect the town to say, “We will meet you halfway.” And expect the town to keep its promise, and to fold it, and to keep it secret, and to fold it again, and to give it to you when you are ready. And when you are ready, you will walk, and you will not be alone, and you will not be afraid, and the world will be kind.

# Notes on the Backwards Road

It felt like a body, and the doctors were hands, and the cartographer was a mind, and the people were blood and breath and the old clock was a heart that had decided to beat in a rhythm that would not scare anyone.

One morning, I woke and found that the paper my mother had left on the table was filled with a map that was not drawn in lines but in a pattern that looked like weather. There was a circle in the center that was the town’s library, and there were lines that moved out like wind, and there were marks that were not marks but stories that had been folded into themselves. I could not read it. I could feel it. The map was a kind of palindrome itself. The center held the past and the edges held the future, and the past and the future were the same distance away, and the town would walk both ways at once.

Maris and I decided to leave. Not because we were afraid of the leaking world, but because we wanted to test the town’s belief that walking backward would meet the future in the past. We packed a small bag, and we took a loaf of bread that the baker gave us, and we took a pear that the gardener gave us, and we took a piece of paper that my mother gave us. We wrote a palindrome on the paper together. It was “now I win.” We wrote it backward as well, “nid I won.” We folded it, and we put it in a pocket, and we stepped out the door, and we walked backward into the world.

Walking backward was harder than we had imagined. Your body wants to look ahead, and it wants to see where you are going, and it wants to step forward into light and away from shadow. But we held each other’s hands and moved, one foot behind the other, and we looked at what we had left, and we found that looking at what you have left makes you feel generous. It makes you feel as if you have given something away and that the thing you have given is a part of you that does not need you to carry it anymore. We walked past the library’s ash door, and we saw that the ash had begun to bloom into small plants that smelled like lemon and like rain. We walked past the market and we saw that the apples had grown into a kind of language that could be spoken without words. We walked past the baker and we heard his voice singing a song that had been forgotten and then remembered.

As we moved, we began to understand that the world’s leaking had not been a failure. It had been a way of telling us that walls do not keep things in or out, and that the world’s boundaries are made of stories and not of stone. The town’s cartographer’s map was an invitation. The old clocks were not counting breaths as a way to tell us that time was slow. They were counting breaths as a way to tell us that time is a body, and that a body is best when it is alive, and that life is a rhythm that can be felt and not merely counted.

We reached the edge of the town, which was not a wall but a place where the ground turned into a soft slope that met the sky, and we stopped. We turned, and we walked forward, and the sky met us. It met us like a friend who had been waiting for you and had forgotten how to call your name but had kept the shape of your name in a memory that was strong and kind. We walked into the world, and the world was not strange. It felt familiar. It felt like a palindrome that had been invented when we were small and we had been told that language has power, and that power must be used with care. We walked toward the future, and we found the past waiting for us, and we shook hands with it, and we said, “We are not afraid.” And the past said, “I know.”

We lived in other towns and other places, and we wrote palindromes in cafés and in train stations and in parks that were made of stone and sand. We listened to echoes and we mapped them, and we taught children how to walk backward without falling, and we taught adults how to speak to the future without making promises they could not keep. We discovered that the world was full of people who loved symmetry and who hated it, and we loved both kinds. We learned that the physics of longing is a science that can be taught by telling stories that begin at the end, and by writing backwards, and by folding time. We learned that the map that my mother had left on the table was not a map of a place but a map of a way of moving.

Years later, we returned to Undisclosed. The town’s old clocks had begun to count minutes again, and the baker had begun to sing, and the gardener had begun to plant seeds in the ground that remembered that it was ready for a new kind of growth. The library had been rebuilt by people who had walked backward into the world and had found a piece of ash in the shape of a door, and they had used it as a foundation for new walls and new books. The cartographer’s work was still visible on the street in the form of echoes that had been folded into lines and into stories and into palindromes that were being told by children who did not understand what they were saying and understood more than anyone.

Maris and I sat at the table that had been used by a doctor who had healed a broken future, and we listened to the room. We pressed our palms against the center of the table, and we felt the presence of people who had sat there, and we let that presence become a memory that belonged to us. We took out the piece of paper that had “now I win” written on it, forward and backward, and we unfolded it carefully, and we read it aloud, and the room changed. It became brighter. It became more patient. It became more willing to love.

We did not rebuild Undisclosed. We did not make it into something it had not been. We let it be. We let it be a place where people write in reverse when they need to, and let it be a place where palindromes are used as prayers and as jokes, and let it be a place where the clocks count breaths when the town wants to remember that time is not a master but a friend. We let it be. And we walked backward again, not away this time, but into the town’s stories, and we walked forward again, not out this time, but into the town’s future, and we found that the past and the future had become the same distance away.

In the end, I understood that the town’s belief was not about moving backward or forward. It was about moving with awareness. It was about knowing that language has edges, and that edges are useful when you are building walls, and that edges are useless when you want to hold someone, and that edges can be made into a kind of bridge if you are careful. It was about the physics of longing, which is a science of giving without keeping and keeping without possessing. It was about the cartographer’s work, which is about mapping the spaces between people and not mapping the people themselves. It was about the doctors’ work, which is about healing future wounds by treating them as present wounds, and about the baker’s work, which is about making bread when bread wants to be made, and about the gardener’s work, which is about planting seeds when seeds want to be planted.

It was about palindromes, which are not only sentences but ways of thinking that make room for symmetry and for asymmetry, for order and for the mess that order cannot hold. It was about writing backward and forward and together and folded, and about letting folded things be secret until they become something that can be told without harm. It was about walking backward into the world and forward into the world and discovering that the world is not two things that are different. It is one thing that changes, and that is the power and the danger and the beauty of it.

If you ever come to Undisclosed, do not expect the town to explain itself. Do not expect the town to apologize for its palindromes and its reverse writing and its backward streets. Do not expect the town to tell you how to read its maps. Expect the town to offer you a piece of paper that will record echoes when you press your fingers against the walls. Expect the town to offer you a palindrome that will make you laugh or think. Expect the town to offer you a pear that tastes patient and gentle. Expect the town to say, “Walk backward. Look at where you have been. Then walk forward. Look at where you are going.” Expect the town to say, “We will meet you halfway.” And expect the town to keep its promise, and to fold it, and to keep it secret, and to fold it again, and to give it to you when you are ready. And when you are ready, you will walk, and you will not be alone, and you will not be afraid, and the world will be kind.
